perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information ” means Information relating to one party (the “Discl osing Party”) and/or the business carried on or proposed or intended to be carried on by that party and which is made available for the purpos es of the Proposal to the other Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of 7 party (the “Receiving Party”) or its Agents by the Discl osing Party or its Agents or recorded in agreed minutes following oral disclosure and any other information otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or it s Agents to the Receiving Party or its Agents, whether before, on or after the date of this Agreement, and whether in writing or otherwise, including any information, anal ysis or specifications derived from, containing or reflecting such information but ex cluding information which:- (i) is publicly available at the time of its disclosu re or becomes publicly available (other than as result of disclosure by the Receiving P arty or any of its Agents contrary to the terms of this letter); or (ii) was lawfully in the possession of the Receiving Pa rty or its Agents (as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonabl e evidence) free of any restriction as to its use or disclosure prior to its being so disclosed; or (iii) following such disclosure, becomes available to the Receiving Party or its Agents (as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonable evidence) from a source other than the Disclosing Part y or its Agents, which source is not bound by any duty of confidentiality owed, directly or indirectly, to the Disclosing Party in relation to such information;
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information ” means Information relating to one party (the “Disclosing Party”) and/or the business carried on or proposed or intended to be carried on by that party and which is made available for the purpos es of the Proposal to the other Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 2 of 7 party (the “Receiving Party”) or its Agents by the Disclosing Party or its Agents or recorded in agreed minutes following oral disclosure and any other information otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or it s Agents to the Receiving Party or its Agents, whether before, on or after the date of this Agreement, and whether in writing or otherwise, including any information, anal ysis or specifications derived from, containing or reflecting such information but ex cluding information which:- (i) is publicly available at the time of its disclosu re; or (ii) was lawfully in the possession of the Receiving Pa rty or its Agents (as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonabl e evidence) free of any restriction as to its use or disclosure prior to its being so disclosed; or (iii) following such disclosure, becomes available to the Receiving Party or its Agents (as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonable evidence) from a source other than the Disclosing Part y or its Agents, which source is not bound by any duty of confidentiality owed, directly or indirectly, to the Disclosing Party in relation to such information;
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'or becomes publicly available (other than as result of disclosure by the Receiving Party or any of its Agents contrary to the terms of this letter)', the definition of Confidential Information now includes information that becomes publicly available *even if* the Receiving Party caused that public disclosure, thus weakening the confidentiality protection. This removal creates an in-text contradiction because the definition now conflicts with the core intent of the NDA, which is to prevent disclosure by the receiving party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party will treat and keep all Confid ential Information as secret and confidential and will not, without the Disclosing Par ty’s written consent, directly or indirectly communicate or disclose (whether in writing or orally or in any other manner) Confidential Information to any other person other than in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Confidential Information' to exclude information that becomes publicly available, *unless* the Receiving Party caused the public disclosure. The changed text removes this exception, meaning that 'Confidential Information' now includes information that became public even due to the Receiving Party's actions. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text,' which states the Receiving Party will keep all Confidential Information secret and not disclose it. By broadening the definition of confidential information to include information the Receiving Party disclosed, even if it is now publicly known, the Receiving Party is now held responsible for information they disclosed and made publicly available, creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.1 The Receiving Party will treat and keep all Confid ential Information as secret and confidential and will not, without the Disclosing Par ty’s written consent, directly or indirectly communicate or disclose (whether in writing or orally or in any other manner) Confidential Information to any other person other than in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1 The Receiving Party will treat and keep all Confid ential Information with reasonable care and will not, without the Disclosing Par ty’s written consent, directly or indirectly communicate or disclose (whether in writing or orally or in any other manner) Confidential Information to any other person other than in accordance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'secret and confidential' with 'reasonable care' weakens the obligation. This creates an in-text contradiction because the contract continues to use the term 'Confidential Information' and assumes a high level of protection, but this clause now allows for a lower standard of care. This contradicts the general intent of the NDA, which is to ensure strict confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Without prejudice to any other rights or remedies of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that damages w ould not be an adequate remedy for any breach by it of the provisions of this A greement and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek the remedie s of injunction, specific performance and other equitable relief for any threat ened or actual breach of any such provision by the Receiving Party or its Agents, an d no proof of special damages shall be necessary for the enforcement of the rights u nder this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the Receiving Party to treat Confidential Information as 'secret and confidential', implying a high level of protection. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'reasonable care'. The contradicted text states that 'damages would not be an adequate remedy for any breach' and that the Disclosing Party is entitled to seek injunctions and specific performance. By lowering the standard of care from 'secret and confidential' to 'reasonable care', the changed text directly contradicts the idea that any breach warrants such strong remedies. 'Reasonable care' is a more easily defensible standard, making it more difficult to prove a breach that warrants injunction or specific performance. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because the remedies outlined in the contradicted text are based on the assumption of a high level of confidentiality, which is undermined by the changed text.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2 The Receiving Party will only use the Confidentia l Information for the sole purpose of technical and commercial discussions between the partie s in relation to the Proposal or for the subsequent performance of any contract betwe en the parties in relation to the Proposal.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 The Receiving Party will only use the Confidentia l Information for the purpose of discussions between the partie s.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'technical and commercial' AND removing 'or for the subsequent performance of any contract between the parties in relation to the Proposal.' drastically narrows the permitted use of the Confidential Information. This creates an in-text contradiction because the NDA might elsewhere assume a broader scope of permitted use related to the potential contract, but this clause now restricts it solely to initial discussions. This directly undermines the purpose of sharing the information, if there is not subsequent work done.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In consideration of each party making available to the other such Information, the parties jointly agree that any dealings between t hem shall be subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement which themselves will be subject to the parameters of the Proposal.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed the Receiving Party to use Confidential Information for 'technical and commercial discussions' and 'subsequent performance of any contract'. The changed text removes 'technical and commercial' and the phrase regarding 'subsequent performance of any contract', severely limiting the permitted use to only 'discussions'. The Contradicted Text states that 'any dealings' are subject to the agreement, implying a broader scope than just initial discussions. The changed text creates a direct contradiction because it restricts the use of information, making it incompatible with the intention of 'any dealings' as stated in the contradicted text.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3 The Receiving Party shall, within seven days of re ceipt of a written demand from the Disclosing Party or of its ceasing to be interested i n the Proposal: (i) Return all written Confidential Information (in cluding all copies); and (ii) expunge or destroy any Confidential Information from any computer, word processor or other device whatsoever into which it was copied, read or programmed by the Receiving Party or on its behalf (in cluding by any person to whom disclosure has been made as permitted under clause 2.3(i) above. The obligations in this clause 3.3 shall not apply to the extent that (but only for so long as) it is necessary to retain copies for the purpo se of providing information to any regulatory authority in accordance with clause 2.3 (ii ) above.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.3 The Receiving Party shall, as soon as reasonably practical after demand from the Disclosing Party, return the Confidential Information. The obligations in this clause 3.3 shall not apply.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'seven days' timeframe and specific destruction requirements while ALSO removing the exception to provide the information to the regulatory authority. This significantly weakens the return-of-information obligations. This creates an in-text contradiction because the contract elsewhere may assume a prompt and complete return/destruction of the information, but this clause allows for ambiguity ('reasonably practical') and essentially eliminates the requirement altogether by removing the sole condition that allow retaining the data. This also creates a problem for clause 2.3(ii), which states that they must cooperate to disclose information required by law.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Notwithstanding clause 2.1, the Receiving Party m ay disclose Confidential Information: (i) To those of its Agents who strictly need to know the Confidential Information for the sole purpose set out in clause 2.2 provided that the Receiving Party shall ensure that such Agents are made aware prior to the disclosure of any part of the Confidential Information that the same is confid ential and that they owe a duty of confidence to the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party shall at all times remain liable for any actions of such Agents that woul d constitute a breach of this Agreement; or (ii) to the extent required by law or the rules of a ny applicable regulatory authority, subject to clause 2.4 below.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in clause 3.3 significantly weakens the obligations regarding the return of confidential information. By removing the 'seven days' timeframe and specific destruction requirements, and eliminating the exception for providing information to regulatory authorities, the modified clause introduces ambiguity with the phrase 'reasonably practical' and effectively eliminates the requirement to return information. This creates a contradiction with the contradicted text, specifically clause 2.3(ii), which states that the receiving party must disclose Confidential Information to the extent required by law. The removal of the original clause 3.3's caveat concerning regulatory authority needs directly conflicts with the ongoing need to disclose under clause 2.3(ii). Therefore, the removal breaks compatibility with other parts of the contract, making it a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Duration The obligations of each party and its Agents under t his Agreement shall [be continuing and shall survive the termination of any d iscussions or negotiations between the parties regarding the Proposal continue for a period of 5 (five) years.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Duration The obligations of each party and its Agents under t his Agreement shall continue for a period of 1 (one) year.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the duration from 5 years to 1 year significantly reduces the time period for which the NDA is effective. This creates an in-text contradiction if other clauses imply or rely on a longer period of confidentiality, such as provisions related to long-term projects or trade secrets with extended lifecycles. Specifically reduces the ability to enforce after a reasonable project timeframe.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In consideration of each party making available to the other such Information, the parties jointly agree that any dealings between t hem shall be subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement which themselves will be subject to the parameters of the Proposal.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the duration of the agreement from 5 years to 1 year. The contradicted text states that the dealings between the parties are subject to the terms and conditions of 'this Agreement' which are themselves subject to the 'Proposal'. If the 'Proposal' or the dealings between the parties require a timeframe longer than 1 year, the reduced duration in the changed text directly contradicts the expectation set by the contradicted text, creating an in-text contradiction as it affects the parameters within which the proposal can be subject.

perturbation.original_text: 8.2 Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be cons trued as prohibiting the Disclosing Party from pursuing any other remedies available by i t, either at law or in equity, for any such threatened or actual breach of this Agreement including specific performance, recovery of damages or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.2 Nothing contained in this Agreement shall be cons trued as prohibiting the Disclosing Party from pursuing any other remedies available by i t, either at law or in equity, for any such threatened or actual breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'including specific performance, recovery of damages or otherwise.' This limits the remedies available to the Disclosing Party in case of a breach. This creates an in-text contradiction to Section 8.1 because now it is unknown what damages could be received after a breach of contract, reducing the effectiveness of injunction, specific performance and other equitable relief. The removal causes the uncertainty in enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Without prejudice to any other rights or remedies of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that damages w ould not be an adequate remedy for any breach by it of the provisions of this A greement and that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek the remedie s of injunction, specific performance and other equitable relief for any threat ened or actual breach of any such provision by the Receiving Party or its Agents, an d no proof of special damages shall be necessary for the enforcement of the rights u nder this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text in 8.2 explicitly included 'specific performance, recovery of damages or otherwise' as remedies available to the Disclosing Party. The changed text omits this phrase, limiting the explicitly stated remedies. This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, which states that 'damages would not be an adequate remedy' and affirms the Disclosing Party's right to 'seek the remedie s of injunction, specific performance and other equitable relief.' The omission in the changed text creates uncertainty regarding the Disclosing Party's ability to seek specific performance or recover damages, directly conflicting with the contradicted text's assurance of such remedies, therefore, according to the definition of 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' a strong contradiction exists.
